caveats: a: OEWS excludes self-employed owners, so owner-performed quoting, sales, engineering, and administration may be underrepresented.
a: Occupation-level wage weighting does not observe task shares within occupations or distinguish already-automated work.
a: The NIST use-case percentages cover U.S. manufacturing generally rather than lower-middle-market machine shops.
rho: The NIST adoption figures cite manufacturing-wide surveys and include chatbots, which do not establish deep workflow implementation.
rho: The McKinsey COO survey covers very large global manufacturers and is directionally relevant but structurally unlike LMM job shops.
rho: Census broadened its AI-use question in November 2025, limiting comparisons with earlier adoption measures.
rho: OSHA citations demonstrate safety and control constraints, not the causal effect of AI on implementation.
e: The injected firm count is itself an estimate and may include firms whose margins or receipts were mis-bridged into the EBITDA band.
e: Establishment-level NAICS classification does not reveal firm-level customer recurrence, owner dependence, or change-of-control restrictions.
e: BizBuySell is a self-selected marketplace and its sold firms are not a representative eligibility sample.
s5: McKinsey's figures are modeled and primarily cover firms with fewer than 500 employees, not the EBITDA-defined lens.
s5: Its sector owner-age statistic combines manufacturing with mining and utilities.
s5: BizBuySell does not provide the total eligible-firm denominator and skews toward smaller, brokered Main Street deals.
s5: Family succession may be a true control transfer, but routine internal reorganizations are excluded from this primitive.
q: No source directly measures how AI-created savings are shared in independent machine-shop contracts.
q: Xometry is a digital intermediary and may overstate price transparency relative to relationship-based direct accounts.
q: Retention varies sharply by certification, capacity scarcity, customer concentration, contract length, and whether savings improve throughput rather than reduce quoted cost.
d5: The exact six-digit real-output series ends in 2023, while the timelier series combines adjacent 3327 industries.
d5: Historical output is volatile and does not identify demand for only recurring outsourced work.
d5: Reshoring expectations are directional and sensitive to policy, exchange rates, capacity, skills, and customer qualification timelines.
d5: The range is a judgmental five-year translation, not a published forecast.
o: There is no direct measure of operator-required quantity for machine shops.
o: The estimate may be lower for simple parts that customers can print or machine internally and higher for certified, difficult-tolerance work.
o: Operator requirement does not imply the current labor model persists; automation can substantially change staffing while leaving accountable ownership intact.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business-to-business job shops principally earn revenue from customer purchase orders or quoted per-job or per-part work; repeat programs can recur, but price, quantity, tolerances, lead time, and quality obligations are commonly reset through new orders or rebids.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal machine departments, shells, non-control financing vehicles, industrial-equipment repair classified outside NAICS 332710, and standardized high-volume turned-product or fastener manufacturing classified outside NAICS 332710.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced machining of metal, plastic, and composite parts on a job or order basis, including associated quoting, process planning, CNC programming, setup, inspection, and production coordination.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS directly measures the industry's occupation mix and wages, not AI-exposed tasks. I wage-weighted the major occupation groups and assigned bounded task exposure within each: more exposure in office, management, sales, engineering, estimating, scheduling, CNC programming, and inspection; much less in physical machining, welding, handling, and maintenance. NIST's reported manufacturing use cases validate the task categories but not their shares. This mapping may overstate exposure because CNC already automates part of production work and may understate exposure where AI-enabled vision, CAM, and scheduling reach frontline tasks.
caveats: OEWS excludes self-employed owners, so owner-performed quoting, sales, engineering, and administration may be underrepresented.
Occupation-level wage weighting does not observe task shares within occupations or distinguish already-automated work.
The NIST use-case percentages cover U.S. manufacturing generally rather than lower-middle-market machine shops.
a.change_evidence: A task-level time-and-wage study across at least 30 independent U.S. job shops, stratified by manual/CNC mix and end market, coding current automation and hours spent on quoting, CAM, scheduling, inspection, documentation, setup, tending, and rework, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The May 2023 OEWS profile reports 265,030 employees in machine shops. Production occupations are 66.65% of employment at a $52,520 annual mean wage, including machinists at 24.18%, CNC operators and programmers at 14.05%, and inspectors at 3.56%. Office support is 9.90%, management 6.83%, business and financial operations 3.18%, and architecture and engineering 2.98%, all with higher mean wages than production. The estimate therefore gives meaningful weight to document, quote, schedule, program, and analyze tasks while keeping the majority of hands-on machine, setup, material, and quality-accountability work outside current AI substitution.

d5.bridge: BLS/FRED measures historical real sectoral output for exact NAICS 332710, and Federal Reserve/FRED measures recent industrial production for the broader NAICS 3327 group; neither is a five-year forecast of constant-quality demand for the lens's current service basket. I treated the exact-industry 2019-2023 near-flat growth as the central anchor, the broader group's 2023-2025 contraction as downside evidence, and NIST's expected reshoring and local-supplier opportunity as upside evidence. Broader 3327 may bias the recent decline away from machine shops, while policy-driven reshoring commentary may bias upside high.
caveats: The exact six-digit real-output series ends in 2023, while the timelier series combines adjacent 3327 industries.
Historical output is volatile and does not identify demand for only recurring outsourced work.
Reshoring expectations are directional and sensitive to policy, exchange rates, capacity, skills, and customer qualification timelines.
The range is a judgmental five-year translation, not a published forecast.
d5.change_evidence: A customer-industry-weighted order forecast using shop ERP backlog, RFQ volume, book-to-bill, and real end-market production forecasts for aerospace, defense, medical, energy, autos, and industrial equipment, matched to NAICS 332710 revenue shares, would materially change year-five demand.
d5.rationale: Exact-industry real sectoral output rose from 102.810 in 2019 to 106.272 in 2023, with a pandemic trough at 92.104 in 2020. The broader machine-shops/turned-products/fasteners industrial-production index then fell from 103.5785 in 2023 to 94.3232 in 2025. This combination supports roughly flat central demand with a wide cyclical range. NIST expects reshoring to create opportunities for smaller domestic suppliers, while global competition, end-market cycles, tariff and input-cost uncertainty, and additive or in-house production can offset that tailwind.

caveats: The injected firm count is itself an estimate and may include firms whose margins or receipts were mis-bridged into the EBITDA band.
Establishment-level NAICS classification does not reveal firm-level customer recurrence, owner dependence, or change-of-control restrictions.
BizBuySell is a self-selected marketplace and its sold firms are not a representative eligibility sample.
e.change_evidence: A representative survey of NAICS 332710 firms in the $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band measuring external revenue, repeat-customer revenue, largest-customer concentration, owner hours, second-line management, certification transferability, and change-of-control clauses would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: NAICS 332710 is unusually aligned with the lens because Census defines machine shops as machining parts on a job or order basis, generally in low-volume work. Most firms in the injected LMM band should therefore serve external customers and have repeat-order potential. The discount removes firms dominated by one-off prototypes, a single non-transferable owner relationship or technical skill set, a highly concentrated customer, or operations that cannot continue under new control without material customer or employee loss. Observed sales of machine-shop and tooling businesses confirm that transferable operations exist, but no source measures the eligible share of the injected LMM population.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $17,327.5M / sectoral output (T30) $46,417.5M, year 2023

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $204,602 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332710); class <5: 8,110 firms, avg revenue/firm $346,793 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $68,041 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,488 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,113,605 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $218,489 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,656 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,426,965 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $476,171 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,368 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,480,026 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,467,581 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 369 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,472,051 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,801,417 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 139 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,455,094 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,760,089 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 2,876

caveats: There is no direct measure of operator-required quantity for machine shops.
The estimate may be lower for simple parts that customers can print or machine internally and higher for certified, difficult-tolerance work.
Operator requirement does not imply the current labor model persists; automation can substantially change staffing while leaving accountable ownership intact.
o.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of customer make-versus-buy decisions and sourcing events, tagged for insourcing, offshore transfer, additive substitution, marketplace fulfillment, autonomous-cell conversion, and supplier-of-record accountability, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: The service basket ends in a physical, tolerance-conforming part made with capital equipment, materials, setup, machine operation, inspection, and delivery, so software alone cannot satisfy most quantity. OSHA's machine-shop record, including citations for machine guarding and hazardous-energy control, reinforces the need for accountable operational control even as AI assists decisions. Some quantity can migrate to customer self-manufacture, offshore suppliers, digital networks, additive manufacturing, or more autonomous cells, but digital marketplaces still coordinate thousands of physical suppliers rather than eliminating them. Accordingly, most year-five demand still requires an accountable machine-shop operator, although the operator may employ fewer indirect labor hours.

caveats: No source directly measures how AI-created savings are shared in independent machine-shop contracts.
Xometry is a digital intermediary and may overstate price transparency relative to relationship-based direct accounts.
Retention varies sharply by certification, capacity scarcity, customer concentration, contract length, and whether savings improve throughput rather than reduce quoted cost.
q.change_evidence: Customer- and part-level quote history from 20-30 independent machine shops, linking realized AI-driven cycle or overhead savings to renewal prices, win rates, customer concentration, contract cost-down clauses, and gross margin over five years, would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Job shops usually quote a fixed job or per-part price before production, so an implemented cost reduction can initially remain with the operator rather than passing through mechanically. Retention erodes as repeat purchase orders are rebid, customers seek productivity concessions, competitors adopt similar tools, and digital marketplaces make pricing and supplier capacity more transparent. Xometry describes a fragmented custom-manufacturing market, digitized quote-to-delivery workflows, pricing outcomes, and a global network of approximately 5,000 active suppliers; those mechanisms support meaningful competitive leakage over five years. The range remains above pure pass-through because quality, lead time, certification, responsiveness, and trusted supplier relationships differentiate many jobs.

rho.bridge: Current adoption and scaling evidence comes from all U.S. businesses and broad manufacturing surveys, not the screened population or the precise exposed task set. I translated the strong intent to invest, the already-visible deployment in production, inventory, and quality, and the low rate of fully embedded operations into the share of exposed opportunity implemented in five years. Large-enterprise manufacturing surveys likely bias implementation upward for small shops; sparse IT/OT data, safety controls, and owner-led change capacity bias it downward, while cheaper packaged quoting, scheduling, and document tools could narrow that gap.
caveats: The NIST adoption figures cite manufacturing-wide surveys and include chatbots, which do not establish deep workflow implementation.
The McKinsey COO survey covers very large global manufacturers and is directionally relevant but structurally unlike LMM job shops.
Census broadened its AI-use question in November 2025, limiting comparisons with earlier adoption measures.
OSHA citations demonstrate safety and control constraints, not the causal effect of AI on implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort study from the NIST MEP National Network reporting shop-level deployment, sustained utilization, labor hours avoided, quality escapes, and implementation failures for independent machine shops under 250 employees would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: NIST reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting to increase use within two years, with reported deployment in manufacturing and production (39%), inventory (33%), and quality (24%). Yet a 2025 survey of manufacturers with at least $1 billion revenue found about two-thirds still at exploration or targeted implementation and only 2% fully embedded across operations; 46% cited data or IT/OT limitations. Census data also show firm-size effects and less than 20% AI use among firms with four or fewer employees. Five years should permit packaged back-office and decision-support deployment, but machine safety, legacy machines, validation, training, and fragmented data constrain full conversion of exposed tasks.

s5.bridge: The sources measure owner age, modeled small-business ownership exits, and marketplace manufacturing transactions rather than five-year qualifying control transfers among eligible LMM machine shops. I used manufacturing's older ownership profile to raise transition pressure above the all-firm baseline, then reduced it because McKinsey finds closure dominates continuity and because specialized equipment, know-how, financing, and owner dependence impede transfers. Marketplace observations undercount confidential and off-market deals but overrepresent owners already willing and able to sell.
caveats: McKinsey's figures are modeled and primarily cover firms with fewer than 500 employees, not the EBITDA-defined lens.
Its sector owner-age statistic combines manufacturing with mining and utilities.
BizBuySell does not provide the total eligible-firm denominator and skews toward smaller, brokered Main Street deals.
Family succession may be a true control transfer, but routine internal reorganizations are excluded from this primitive.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-matched five-year transaction panel for NAICS 332710 from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, and broker interviews, deduplicated and tagged for control transfer, closure, family transfer, and EBITDA band, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: McKinsey reports that more than 60% of owners in capital-intensive sectors including manufacturing are over 55 and about 26% are over 65, while specialized equipment, technical knowledge, and working capital make succession harder. Its model projects six million small-business ownership exits by 2035 but only more than one million viable transfer candidates, and reports that 92% of 2022 SMB exits were closures versus 5% sales and 3% transfers to new owners. At the same time, BizBuySell analyzed 2,303 sold manufacturing listings for its five-year benchmarks and reported manufacturing acquisitions rose 15% in 2024, demonstrating a functioning transfer market. The base reflects material retirement pressure without treating every retirement, closure, listing, or internal reorganization as a qualifying control transfer.

principal_driver: Repeat job history plus labor-intensive quoting, programming, scheduling, inspection, and production coordination creates a credible AI-assisted capacity and avoided-hiring opportunity around scarce skilled labor.
principal_weakness: Most labor still supports physical production, implementation is constrained by legacy systems and safety-critical workflows, and competitive rebidding can pass common productivity gains to customers.
